1.blurb: King Seong of Baekje, who sent the 538 Buddha gift, is the central figure of Ancient Korea Chapter 6 — the Baekje king who completed his kingdom's Buddhist conversion, moved the capital to Sabi, and was killed in 554 fighting the rival Korean kingdom of Silla. From the Korean side, the Buddha statue Seong sent to Japan was a piece of standard Baekje diplomacy: project soft power through religion to keep your neighbors as allies.

Buddhism reached Han China in the first century CE along the Silk Road from the Kushan Empire — a process explored in Han Dynasty Chapter 7. The Han received Buddhism slowly: an emperor's dream, a few foreign monks, a handful of translated sutras. By the time the religion reached Japan in 538, it had been digesting in the Chinese world for half a millennium.

Ashoka's Maurya state was the first political power to use government machinery to spread Buddhism — the topic of Maurya Empire Chapter 5. Ashoka dispatched missionary monks to Sri Lanka, Greek-controlled Bactria, and probably as far as Egypt. The infrastructure that eventually carried Buddhism to Japan a thousand years later was created by Ashoka's third-century-BCE state-Buddhist project.

The Buddha lived around 480 BCE in the late Vedic period of northern India — Vedic Period Chapter 7 covers his career and the explosion of new religious movements that emerged in the same generation. The religion that would arrive in Japan two thousand years later was already by Buddha's lifetime a reformist response to entrenched Vedic ritual practice.

The Kushan Empire of the first to third centuries CE was the political vehicle that carried Buddhism out of India and across Central Asia, creating the Silk Road monastic networks that eventually transmitted the religion to China — the story told in Post-Maurya Kingdoms Chapter 5. Without the Kushans, Buddhism would have stayed Indian.

The three Korean kingdoms of Goguryeo, Baekje, and Silla — covered in Ancient Korea Chapter 4 — were the immediate political world the Yamato court interacted with. Baekje was Japan's closest ally and primary Buddhist conduit; Silla was Japan's regional rival; Goguryeo loomed in the north as the great power both Japan and Tang China had to consider when planning Korean policy.

The Buddhist transformation of Chinese civilization during the Six Dynasties period (Six Dynasties Chapter 5) is the precondition for everything that followed. By the time Korean Baekje sent the Buddha to Japan in 538, China had already been digesting Buddhism for four centuries, producing the great translation projects, monastic institutions, and theological literature that would all flow on through Korea to Japan.

2.blurb: The Sui dynasty (581–618), covered in Sui, Tang & Song China Chapter 1, was the short-lived continental superpower that Shotoku Taishi was modeling Japan on. The Sui reunified China after centuries of fragmentation, built the Grand Canal, codified the Kaihuang legal system, and provided the bureaucratic template that the Twelve Cap Ranks system and Seventeen-Article Constitution were attempting to import.

Justinian I — covered in Byzantine Empire Chapter 2 — provides the cleanest cross-civilizational comparison to Shotoku Taishi. Both ruled in close partnership with strong female imperial figures (Theodora and Suiko). Both built signature monumental religious architecture (Hagia Sophia and Horyu-ji). Both attempted to import the institutional model of a more sophisticated continental neighbor. Both ran out of time before their projects could fully consolidate.

The Sassanid Empire of Persia, the dominant power of western Asia for four centuries, was at the height of its final century during Shotoku's regency — covered in Persian Empire Chapter 10. Khosrow I's Sassanid court was, like Shotoku's Yamato court, attempting to balance the import of foreign cultural and religious material against the preservation of indigenous traditions.

When the Sui collapsed in 618, the Tang dynasty inherited the Sui institutional infrastructure and pushed it further — Sui, Tang & Song China Chapter 2 covers the Tang founding under Li Yuan and Li Shimin's Zhenguan-era reforms. From the founding of Horyu-ji onward, Tang China was the primary cultural model for the entire Asuka-Nara reform program.

The Hagia Sophia in Constantinople — Justinian's signature building project, covered in Byzantine Empire Chapter 2 — is the closest contemporary parallel to Horyu-ji. Both were monumental religious buildings completed within seventy years of each other (Hagia Sophia 537, Horyu-ji 607), both were attempts by middle-rank powers to build at the architectural scale of a more sophisticated continental neighbor, and both have survived as living buildings to the present day.

3.blurb: Tang Taizong's Zhenguan-era reforms, covered in Sui, Tang & Song China Chapter 2, are the direct model for what Naka no Oe and Kamatari were attempting in the Taika Reform two decades later. Both rulers used a coup-and-reform sequence — Xuanwu Gate Incident of 626, Isshi Incident of 645 — followed by an aggressive program of bureaucratic centralization.

The Xuanwu Gate Incident of 626 (Sui, Tang & Song China Chapter 2) was the bloody palace coup that brought Tang Taizong to power, killing his brothers and forcing his father's abdication. The structural parallel to the Isshi Incident of 645 is striking: both were coups in which a junior royal killed the senior prince to clear the way for a Tang-modeled reform program.

3.matchText: destroyed by a coalition of Tang Chinese armies
3.blurb: The destruction of Baekje in 660 — Ancient Korea Chapter 7 — is told from the Korean side as the opening move of the Great Unification War, when Tang and Silla allied to dismantle the Three Kingdoms in sequence. Goguryeo would fall in 668, and then Silla would expel Tang armies in 676 to unite the peninsula on its own terms.

The Battle of Baekgang in 663 — covered from the Silla side in Ancient Korea Chapter 7 — is the maritime engagement at the mouth of the Geum River where the Tang-Silla coalition annihilated the Yamato-Baekje fleet. The Korean accounts emphasize the strategic significance: with Japanese intervention crushed, the unification of Korea under Silla became inevitable.

3.matchText: Tang and Silla turned on each other in the post-conquest carve-up
3.blurb: After defeating Baekje and Goguryeo together, Tang and Silla turned on each other for control of the peninsula — a war Silla won by 676, expelling Tang armies and unifying Korea. Ancient Korea Chapter 7 covers the war's twists: a Silla king who alternately fought and negotiated, a Tang court that overcommitted to other frontiers, and a peninsula that emerged as a single Korean state for the first time.

The Rashidun Caliphate (the four 'rightly-guided' caliphs from 632 to 661) was contemporary with the Isshi Incident and the Taika Reform — Umayyad Caliphate Chapter 3 covers the period from the Arab side. The same years Naka no Oe was building a Tang-modeled state at Asuka, the Rashidun were destroying the Sassanid Empire and conquering Byzantine Egypt and the Levant.

4.blurb: Wu Zetian, the only woman in Chinese history to formally hold the title of Huangdi — Sui, Tang & Song China Chapter 2 — was contemporary with Empress Jito and ruled in much the same period. The two female monarchs offer a useful contrast: where Jito's reign was treated as institutionally normal in the Yamato pattern of female sovereigns, Wu Zetian's reign was treated as a singular aberration by every later Chinese historian.

The Tang Chinese capital at Chang'an under Emperor Xuanzong (Sui, Tang & Song China Chapter 3) was the cosmopolitan reference point Hakuho-era Japanese embassies were bringing back to the Yamato court. Tang Chang'an's million-person population, multi-ethnic neighborhoods, Persian and Sogdian merchants, and Buddhist monastic networks were the model of what an East Asian imperial capital looked like.

The Umayyad Caliphate (Umayyad Caliphate Chapter 4) was the rapidly expanding Arab empire of the late seventh and early eighth centuries — by 711, Umayyad armies would conquer al-Andalus in the west and reach the Indus River in the east. The Tenmu-Jito state-formation moment in Japan was contemporary with one of the most explosive imperial expansions in world history.

Unified Silla, covered in Ancient Korea Chapter 8, was Japan's most important continental contemporary across the entire Hakuho and Nara periods. Silla's golden age (roughly 668 to 935) was a 250-year stretch of cultural flowering, Buddhist patronage, maritime trade reaching as far as the Persian Gulf, and intermittently strained relations with Japan over the Baekje refugee question.

5.blurb: Tang Chang'an at the time of Heijo-kyo's founding was the largest city in the world (around one million residents) and the most cosmopolitan — Sui, Tang & Song China Chapter 3 covers its multi-ethnic neighborhoods, Sogdian merchants, Indian Buddhist monks, and the wave of Korean and Japanese students moving through the capital. Heijo-kyo was a deliberate quarter-scale copy of this model.

6.blurb: The Abbasid Revolution of 750 — Islamic Golden Age Chapter 1 — overthrew the Umayyad Caliphate and established the dynasty that would build Baghdad in 762 as a planned capital on a Tang-influenced grid plan eerily similar to Heijo-kyo's. The Daibutsu was dedicated in 752, two years after the revolution; the two great planned capitals of the late eighth century were being built within a decade of each other on opposite ends of Eurasia.

Borobudur in central Java, one of the largest Buddhist monuments ever built (Srivijaya Chapter 1), was begun a generation after the Daibutsu was cast — the Southeast Asian counterpart to Japan's great state-Buddhist building project. Both monuments demonstrate the eighth-century moment when Mahayana Buddhism was being patronized at imperial scale across most of east and southeast Asia.

The Tang High Tang period of Emperor Xuanzong's reign (Sui, Tang & Song China Chapter 3) is the cultural moment Ganjin sailed from. The Tang temple architecture that Toshodai-ji preserves — and that no longer exists in Tang China itself, since the original Tang wooden buildings burned down centuries ago — flowed to Japan through monks like Ganjin in the years just before the An Lushan Rebellion broke the Tang.

7.blurb: The An Lushan Rebellion of 755–763 (Sui, Tang & Song China Chapter 4) killed perhaps 36 million people and broke the back of the Tang dynasty. By the 770s when the Hyakumanto Darani was being printed and the Dokyo Affair was unfolding, Tang China was a visibly weakening power — and the kentoshi mission that had brought Japan its institutional models for two centuries was running on diminishing returns from the Tang side.

The Dokyo Affair has a structural parallel in the Byzantine Empire's ongoing tensions between iconoclast emperors and the powerful monastic establishment — Byzantine Empire Chapter 7 covers the iconoclast century when the Byzantine state was struggling with exactly the question that Dokyo's near-coup raised in Japan: how much political power should organized monastic religion be allowed to accumulate within a state that had patronized it?

The Heian period that succeeds the Asuka-Nara periods (Heian Japan Chapter 1) covers 794 to 1185 — the brilliant aesthetic civilization centered on the new capital at Kyoto, the Fujiwara clan's regency dominance, the masterworks of Murasaki Shikibu and Sei Shonagon, and the eventual rise of the warrior class that would close the classical era in the Genpei War.

8.blurb: Heian-kyo, the new capital Kammu founded in 794, is the central setting of Heian Japan Chapter 1 onward. The city would remain the imperial capital for the next 1,074 years — until the Meiji Restoration in 1868 — and would be the stage for the Heian court culture, the Fujiwara regency, the brilliant women writers, and eventually the Genpei War that ended the classical period.

The Kyoto that Kammu founded as Heian-kyo became the center of one of the most refined and aesthetically obsessed court cultures in human history — Heian Japan Chapter 4 covers the Heian court aesthetic in detail. Murasaki Shikibu's Tale of Genji, Sei Shonagon's Pillow Book, and the entire kana-script literary explosion happen in the city Kammu founded in 794.

Charlemagne was crowned Emperor of the Romans in 800, six years after Kammu founded Heian-kyo — the Carolingian moment is the European structural parallel to the Heian moment. Both Kammu and Charlemagne were attempting to consolidate strong centralized monarchies after centuries of fragmentation; both founded capitals (Aachen and Heian-kyo) that would symbolize their dynastic ambitions; both used religious institutions (the Frankish church for Charlemagne, the new Heian temples for Kammu) as instruments of imperial authority.

The Viking raid on Lindisfarne in 793, one year before Kammu founded Heian-kyo, is the conventional opening of the Viking Age (Viking Age Chapter 1). The same year Japan's last classical capital was being founded, the European world was opening one of its most disruptive periods of long-distance maritime expansion — the Scandinavian raids that would reshape the political geography of England, Ireland, and the North Atlantic across the next two and a half centuries.

Harun al-Rashid (786–809), the Abbasid caliph at the height of Baghdad's golden age, was the eastern-Mediterranean contemporary of Emperor Kammu — Islamic Golden Age Chapter 2 covers his reign and the House of Wisdom translation project. The Baghdad of Harun al-Rashid and the Heian-kyo of Kammu were the two great planned capitals of the late eighth century, both at the height of their cultural production.

The samurai class that the Emishi-war military reorganization of Kammu's reign began producing would dominate Japanese politics from the late Heian period onward — Heian Japan Chapter 6 covers the rise of the warrior aristocracy. The institutional foundation laid by Kammu's abolition of the conscript army in 792 produced the warrior families whose descendants would close the classical era in the Genpei War four centuries later.
